food_name: Dry Noodle Tossed in Special Sauce
aliases: Dry Noodle Tossed in Special Sauce

english: Dry Noodle Tossed in Special Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for dry tossed noodles with sauce; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta and noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for dry-tossed noodle dish with sauce exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific noodle preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of dry tossed noodles is approximately 250 g, equivalent to one restaurant bowl as typically served at Din Tai Fung and similar Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, cooked, with mixed sauce — adapted composite estimate based on HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles and soy-sesame sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'dry tossed noodles with special sauce' as a composite dish. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for cooked wheat noodles (plain) and estimated sauce contribution based on USDA SR Legacy data for soy sauce, sesame oil, and chilli oil at typical restaurant proportions (~15 g sauce per 100 g dish). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with published Singapore food composition data for similar noodle dishes. Sodium is elevated due to soy-based sauce.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat, boiled — Glycemic Index value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to dry-tossed wheat noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 53 (low) for boiled wheat noodles. Dry-tossed preparation does not substantially alter the starch digestibility of the noodle base; the sauce components (soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil) contribute negligible additional glycemic load. GI of 53 applied as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 71.25) / 100 ≈ 37.8, rounded to 38.2 accounting for sauce carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1200 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 71 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 38, which is high; portion control to half a serving and pairing with non-starchy vegetables or protein is recommended to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from sesame and chilli oils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content and moderate fat contribution from sesame and chilli oils; while saturated fat is relatively low at approximately 0.7 g per 100 g, the overall sodium burden warrants caution, and reduced-sauce preparation or smaller portions are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Noodle Tossed in Special Sauce (干拌面, Gān Bàn Miàn) is a Chinese noodle dish in which freshly boiled wheat noodles are drained and tossed — rather than served in broth — with a fragrant sauce typically composed of soy sauce, sesame oil, chilli oil, vinegar, and garlic, then finished with chopped spring onions. The dish is a staple of Chinese cuisine and is widely served in Singapore at restaurants ranging from casual hawker stalls to premium chains such as Din Tai Fung. The texture is springy and chewy from the wheat noodles, while the sauce delivers a complex umami-forward, slightly nutty, and mildly spicy flavour profile. Because the noodles are served without soup, the sauce coating is more concentrated and flavourful than a comparable soupy noodle dish. Nutritionally, the dish is carbohydrate-dominant, providing approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~5.8 g) and fat (~4.5 g) contributions. Sodium content is notably elevated due to the soy-based sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the wheat noodle base is approximately 53 (low), but the glycemic load per standard serving is high due to portion size, requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is typically vegetarian but may not be vegan depending on sauce formulation, and contains gluten and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dry noodle with sauce?
Is 干拌面 suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of dry noodles with soy sauce?
Is Din Tai Fung dry noodle vegetarian?
What is the GI of wheat noodles?
Dry noodle vs soup noodle nutrition difference
Can I eat dry tossed noodles if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish as served at Din Tai Fung Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked wheat noodles and USDA SR Legacy data for sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, the source data is from approved primary sources, and the resulting values are consistent with published Singapore food composition literature for comparable noodle dishes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on HPB Foodo cooked wheat noodle data and USDA SR Legacy sauce component data; no exact composite entry was found in any approved primary source for this specific dish. 2. Sodium estimate of 480 mg per 100 g reflects typical soy-sauce-based noodle dish sodium levels in Singapore food composition data; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant and sauce formulation. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this dish composite. 4. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo noodle entries used. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this dish is plant-based and B12 is not reported in the source data. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source composite. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source data. 10. trans_fat_g is 0.0 and cholesterol_mg is 0.0; these are confirmed zero for a plant-oil and wheat-noodle based dish with no animal fat components per USDA SR Legacy data. 11. Halal status is listed as false as Din Tai Fung Singapore is not halal-certified; individual sauce ingredients may contain non-halal components. 12. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish preparation was found.
